content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

October 23, 1992

Honorable Barbara Siegler
City Councilmember
City of Windsor
107 Windsor Palms Drive
Windsor, CA 95492

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-92-508

Dear Councilmember Siegler:

This is in response to your letter requesting advice as a
Windsor City Councilmember regarding your responsibilities under
the conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the "Act").¹

Please note that nothing in this letter should be construed
to evaluate any conduct which may have already taken place. In
addition, this letter is based on the facts presented to us. The
Commission does not act as the finder of fact in providing advice.
(In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

May you participate in Windsor City Council decisions
regarding subdivisions in the jurisdiction and ordinances
affecting building permits in the jurisdiction where you are
employed by the North American Mortgage Company which originates,

_______________
¹ Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95812 • (916) 322-5660

Our File No. A-92-508
Page 2


sells, and services loans and has done and will do business in
Windsor?

CONCLUSION

You are required to disqualify yourself from participating in
any governmental decision in which the North American Mortgage
Company is directly involved. You are also required to disqualify
yourself from any other decision which will foreseeably increase
or decrease the gross revenues of the company for a fiscal year,
or the assets or liabilities of the company by $250,000 or more,
or will increase or decrease its expenses by $100,000 or more.

FACTS

You are a member of the Windsor City Council and, in your
private capacity, are employed with the North American Mortgage
Company (the "NAMC") as a computer programer/analyst.

The NAMC has 42 production offices in seven states and is
publicly traded on the New York Stock Exchange. The NAMC
originates, sells, and services loans. The NAMC receives 1.5
percent in points and fees on loans and a similar amount if the
NAMC sells a loan. The NAMC has done and will continue to do
business in your jurisdiction. According to the prospectus of the
NAMC you submitted with your request, the total revenue for the
first quarter of 1992 was 41.5 million dollars.

You stated that your position with the NAMC as a computer
programer/analyst is a salaried position. You do not participate
in or receive commissions from any of the loans made by the NAMC.
However, you stated that you do own stock in the NAMC purchased
through an employee stock option plan.

The Windsor City Council regularly considers subdivision
approvals and ordinances regulating the issuance of building
permits in the jurisdiction. You stated that on October 28, 1992,
the city council will consider a building permit allocation
ordinance that will affect development in the city. The ordinance
would lift a moratorium on building permits that currently exist
and permit a limited number of building permits to be issued with
specified restrictions.

You have asked whether your various interests in the NAMC
creates a conflict of interest with respect to the ordinance
decision and future development decisions.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making, or in any way attempting to influence a
governmental decision in which the official knows or has reason to

Our File No. A-92-508
Page 3


Know he or she has a financial interest.  Section 87103 specifies
that a public official has a financial interest in a decision if
it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from the effect on the
public generally, on the official or a member of his or her
immediate family or on:

     (a)  Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect investment worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

                        * * *

     (c)  Any source of income ... aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by, or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when
the decision is made.

     (d)  Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

               Section 87103(a), (c) and (d).

     You are employed with the NAMC as a computer programmer/
analyst.  Thus, the NAMC is an economic enterprise pursuant to
Section 87103(d).  We also assume that the NAMC has been a source of
income to you of $250 or more, making the NAMC a "source of
income" as set forth in Section 87103(c).  This constitutes a
second basis for finding the NAMC is your economic interest.
Consequently, you are required to disqualify yourself from any
decision of the city council which may foreseeably have a material
financial effect on the NAMC distinguishable from the effect on
the public generally.

Foreseeability

     Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reason-
ably foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made
depends on the facts of each particular case.  An effect is
considered reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial
likelihood that it will occur.  Certainty is not required.

___________________
2  You also stated you own stock in the NAMC.  If the stock is
worth $1,000 or more, this investment interest would also
constitute an economic interest under the Act.  However, as
discussed above, even absent the investment interest, you will
still have an economic interest in the NAMC due to your receipt of
income and position with the NAMC.

Our File No. A-92-508
Page 4


However, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.)

     1.  Specific Developments

     The Thorner Opinion (supra) concerned requests for water
variances for property slated for development. The Commission
concluded that such decisions would foreseeably affect business
entities that contract with or supply goods to developers if the
business entities had bid on a developer's project, were preparing
to bid on the project, or were likely to supply goods.

     This rationale would appear to apply to your facts as well.
If the city council is considering a project in which the NAMC has
an interest, or will likely be involved in the project in the
future, it will be foreseeable that the NAMC will be financially
affected by the subdivision decision. Where there is no current
connection, or contemplated connection with the subdivision, it is
not foreseeable that the decision on the specific subdivision will
have a financial effect on the NAMC.

     Of course this rule would be different if the developer in
question had some past connection with the NAMC that suggested
that future business resulting from the development would be
obtained by the NAMC. (In re Thorner, at p. 9.) Similarly, this
rule would be different where there was not substantial
competition in the jurisdiction regarding such loans. (In re
Thorner, at pp. 10 and 11.) For example, if the NAMC was the only
loan marketing company that operated in the city, or held a
predominant share of the market, it would be foreseeable that
independent subdivision approvals would have a financial effect on
the NAMC. Of course, this is ultimately a factual determination
that must be made by you and your city attorney.

     2.  The Ordinance

     Conversely, a decision that would affect all development in
the jurisdiction, such as the ordinance that limits building
permits, would foreseeably affect all the mortgage companies in
the jurisdiction, including the NAMC.

     In Thorner, the Commission also considered the effect of
lifting a water moratorium on an official's business in the
jurisdiction. The Commission stated that since the building
activity within the district would increase as a result of a
decision to lift the moratorium and the increase in building
activities would provide significant opportunities for the
official's business to increase its sales within the district, it
was foreseeable that the official's business would be financially

Our File No. A-92-508
Page 5


affected.³ (In re Thorner, at p. 10.) This analysis would
suggest that it is also foreseeable that the Windsor ordinance
will foreseeably financially affect the NAMC.

Materiality

Regulation 18702.1 provides that the effect of a decision is
material if any business entity in which the public official is an
officer, director, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any
position of management, is directly involved⁴ in the decision
before the public official's agency.

However, you have asked about situations where the NAMC would
not be directly involved, but would only be indirectly affected.
Regulation 18702.2 provides guidelines regarding when an indirect
effect on a business entity is material. Whether the financial
effect of a decision on a business entity is material depends on
its financial size.

You stated that the NAMC is listed on the New York Stock
Exchange. Regulation 18702.2(a) provides that for such
businesses, the financial effect of a decision is material if:

     (1) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease to the gross revenues for a fiscal year
of $250,000 or more...; or

     (2) The decision will result in the business
entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or
reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a
fiscal year in the amount of $100,000 or more...;
or

     (3) The decision will result in an increase
or decrease in the value of assets or liabilities
of $250,000 or more....


_________________________

³ The Commission also concluded the effect would be material.
However, this conclusion was based on prior versions of the
materiality regulations. Materiality under current law is
discussed below.

⁴ The NAMC is directly involved in a decision before the city
council when the NAMC or the NAMC's agent: (1) initiates the
proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing an
application, claim, appeal, or similar request of; (2) is a named
party in the proceeding; or, (3) is the subject of the proceeding
because the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval,
denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other entitlement
to, or contract with, the NAMC. (Regulation 18702.1(b).)

Our File No. A-92-508
Page 6


Thus, you are required to disqualify yourself from participating in any decision which could foreseeably increase or decrease
the gross revenues for a fiscal year,5 assets or liabilities of
the NAMC by $250,000 or more, or increase or decrease expenses by
$100,00 or more.

1. Specific Developments

For example, even if you determine that the financial effect
on the NAMC resulting from a decision on a specific subdivision is
foreseeable, it may not be material pursuant to the standards set
forth above. However, if the effect of a new subdivision on the
NAMC's gross revenues, expenses, assets or liabilities would reach
the thresholds described above, you would be required to
disqualify yourself from the decision.

2. The Ordinance

The same is true with respect to the materiality of the
effect of the ordinance on the NAMC. For example, if the NAMC's
market share in Windsor is relatively small, the foreseeable
financial effect caused by the decision may be below the
thresholds in Regulation 18702.2.

Conversely, if the NAMC services a large percentage of the
market in Windsor, it may be foreseeable that any increase or
decrease in city-wide building would impact the NAMC materially.
However, these are actual questions that we cannot determine.6




5 In the Atigh Advice letter (No. I-86-270) we advised that
"gross revenues" as used in Regulation 18702.2, meant "all
revenues from sales, and all contracts, grants and other payments.
An increase or decrease in the number of customers served by the
[business] would affect the [business'] gross revenues. Overhead
expenses and other costs of producing the revenues are not
subtracted from the gross revenues." Thus, gross revenue includes
all revenue to NAMC in the fiscal year from all sources. From the
information in the prospectus you enclosed it appears that the
NAMC's gross revenues for a fiscal year would be approximately 166
million dollars (41.5 million per quarter).

6 The Act provides an exception where decisions affect the
interest of an official in the same manner as the public
generally, or a significant segment of the public generally.
However, under your facts, the effect on the business entity, the
NAMC, and would not be shared by a significant segment of the
population of the jurisdiction.

Our File No. A-92-508
Page 7


Other Decisions

     While under some circumstances a series of decisions may be
too interrelated to be considered separately (Miller Advice
Letter, No. A-82-119), generally, most decisions may be analyzed
independently to determine if there will be a foreseeable material
financial effect on your interests. (In re Owen (1976) 2 FPPC
Ops. 77.)

     Thus, where a decision solely concerns a single phase of a
development, you may analyze the foreseeable financial effect
caused by that phase to determine if you have a conflict of
interest. However, where the subdivision as a whole may be
dependent on the phase, then you would be required to disqualify
yourself if the subdivision as a whole would have a material
financial effect on your economic interests. Of course, this
determination is necessarily dependent on the specific facts of
the subdivision decision.

     If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.7

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [signature]
                                        By: John W. Wallace
                                            Counsel, Legal Division

SH:JWW:aa


_____________________________
7     Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries. Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 10¢ per page.
